title: France will be the Eurozone’s next economic underperformer
gist: Oxford Economics forecasts France becoming the Eurozone's next laggard, with GDP growth of just 0.5% in 2026 and 0.7% in 2027 as fiscal fragility, political instability, and a deteriorating labour market weigh on consumption; only export manufacturing in defence and aeronautics offers resilience.

themes.theme: France growth forecast and fiscal/political drag

insights.claim: Oxford Economics projects French GDP growth of just 0.5% in 2026 and 0.7% in 2027, positioning France as the Eurozone's next economic underperformer.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: A sub-1% growth trajectory for the Eurozone's second-largest economy is a drag on regional aggregate growth and argues for caution on French sovereign risk and euro-area cyclical exposure.
insights.quote: France is poised to become the Eurozone's next economic underperformer, with GDP growth projected at a mere 0.5% in 2026 and 0.7% in 2027.
insights.locator: France is poised to become the

insights.claim: A fraught fiscal position and fractured politics in France could reduce GDP growth by roughly 0.3 percentage points annually from 2027 to 2030.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: A persistent ~0.3pp annual growth haircut through 2030 compounds into a materially weaker medium-term outlook, relevant for OAT spreads and French debt sustainability.
insights.quote: which could reduce GDP growth by approximately 0.3 percentage points annually from 2027 to 2030

insights.claim: French household consumption is under pressure from a labour-market downturn, worsened by political instability and external shocks such as the recent Middle East conflict.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Consumption is the swing factor in the French outlook; watch labour data and confidence surveys for confirmation before adding domestic consumer exposure.

insights.claim: France's labour market is deteriorating with rising unemployment and stagnant real wages, driving weak consumer confidence and elevated household saving rates.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: High precautionary savings and flat real wages cap any near-term consumption-led recovery, a headwind for French domestic-facing equities and retail.
insights.quote: France's labour market is deteriorating, with rising unemployment and stagnant real wages contributing to weak consumer confidence and high household saving rates.

insights.claim: Export-oriented manufacturing, particularly defence and aeronautics, offers France some resilience against an otherwise bleak growth outlook.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: The divergence points to relative value in French defence and aerospace exporters versus domestic-demand-dependent names as consumption stays weak.
insights.quote: While export-oriented manufacturing, particularly in defence and aeronautics, offers some resilience, the overall outlook remains bleak.
